content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
C HAIRM A N
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
July 2, 1997
Walter Bowman
6086 Barry Drive
Cypress, California 90630
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-327
Dear Mr. Bowman:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Some of your questions were too general or ambiguous to be
answered or related to past conduct involving a third party.
The Act requires the Commission to provide written advice to any person whose duties
under the Act are in question or to that person’s authorized representative. (Section 83114(b).)
The Commission does notprovide advice to thirdparties regarding public officials’ duties under
the Act. (Regulation 18329(b)(8).) Moreover, the Commission does not give advice relating to
past conduct. As such, nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct
which may already have taken place. Further, this letter is based on the facts as they have been
presented to us. The Commission does not act as finder of fact in providing advice. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Consequently, although we are unable to provide written assistance with respect to the
issues you raise in your letter, we have enclosed a copy of the Act for your information. We
have also provided some general assistance with respect to your conflict of interest inquiries.
2
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329, subd. (c)(3).)
428 1 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-327
Page 2
ANALYSIS
You askfa city councilmember can speak to an issue as a private citizen andfhe or she
can vote on an issuefor which he or she has solicited opposition.
Included within the Act are conflict-of-interest provisions intended to insure that public
officials, whether elected or appointed, will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free
from any bias attributable to personal financial interests, or to the financial interests ofpersons
who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) To further this purpose, Section 87100 provides:
“No public official, at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he
has a financial interest.”
A “public official” is defined by the Act to include every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.) A public official “makes” or
“participates in making a governmental decision” when he or she votes on, approves, or
otherwise makes use ofhis or her official position to influence the outcome of a governmental
decision. (Regulations 18700 (b) and (c); 18700.1.)
Although city councilmembers are prohibited from participating in matters outlined
above, there is no barrier to appearances before the city council as a member ofthe general
public, i.e. he or she appears and speaks in his or her capacity as an individual citizen,
representing himself or herself on matters related solely to his or herpersonal interests. The
right of officials to speak as individual citizens on matters ofpurely personal interest is protected
by Regulations 18700(d)(2) and 18700.1(b)(1). It is important, however, that the official avoid
giving the impression that he or she speaks in the interest of any person or group other than
himself or herself and his or her immediate family, or that he or she is acting in any official
capacity. (Harper Advice Letter, No. A-96-298.)
Additionally, the enclosed booklet sets forth the specific financial interests which would
lead an official to have a disqualifying conflict of interest.


File No. 1-97-327
Page 3
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Meena Patel
Graduate Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:MP:tls

sections.question: Can a city councilmember speak to an issue as a private citizen and can he or she vote on an issue for which he or she has solicited opposition?
sections.conclusion: Although city councilmembers are prohibited from participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest, there is no barrier to appearances before the city council as a member of the general public when speaking solely on matters related to personal interests. However, officials must avoid giving the impression they speak for others or in official capacity. The specific financial interests creating a disqualifying conflict are set forth in the enclosed materials.

sections.question_synthetic: Can a city councilmember appear and speak before the city council as a private citizen on personal matters, and can they vote on issues where they have solicited opposition?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: City councilmembers may speak before the city council as private citizens on personal matters, provided they clearly represent only themselves and avoid suggesting they act in official capacity or on behalf of others. Conflict of interest restrictions apply to voting on matters where the official has financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is informal advice letter with limited question and conclusion sections. Some questions deemed too general/ambiguous or relating to third parties and past conduct were declined. Letter includes regulatory framework discussion rather than single direct answer. File number indicates 1997 letter (1-97-327).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Can a city councilmember speak to an issue as a private citizen and can he or she vote on an issue for which he or she has solicited opposition?

CONCLUSION: Although city councilmembers are prohibited from participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest, there is no barrier to appearances before the city council as a member of the general public when speaking solely on matters related to personal interests. However, officials must avoid giving the impression they speak for others or in official capacity. The specific financial interests creating a disqualifying conflict are set forth in the enclosed materials.

embedding.summary: In response to a request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions under California's Political Reform Act, the FPPC explains that city councilmembers can appear and speak before the city council as private citizens on personal matters, but must avoid any impression of official capacity or representation of outside interests.
